title: What Is Code?
body:
What Is Code?

By Srujana Pundi, May 2026

When I tell people outside tech that I work with code, their eyes glaze over. Code is perceived as something esoteric, known only to a select few who speak a language no one else understands. But the more I think about it, the more I believe code is something much more universal. Code is a vocabulary for thought. It is the language we use to capture conceptual models, express intent, and communicate ideas in a way that is precise enough for a machine to execute. And whether we realise it or not, we all use code in some form or another.

In this article, I explore what code really is, how it shapes the way we think, and why it matters more than ever in the age of AI.

Two Aspects of Code

To understand what code is, it helps to look at it from two angles: what it says and what it does. Code as a vocabulary for conceptual models is about what it says. Code as an executable specification is about what it does.

Code as a Vocabulary for Conceptual Models

Code is a structured vocabulary that captures a conceptual model of a domain. A domain is just a sphere of knowledge, activity, or interest — like banking, healthcare, or e-commerce. When we write code for a domain, we are essentially creating a vocabulary to describe that domain precisely.

Consider a banking application. The domain includes concepts like accounts, transactions, balances, interest rates, and customers. When we write code for this domain, we create classes, functions, and variables that correspond to these concepts. The code becomes a vocabulary for talking about banking operations.

This vocabulary is not just for the computer. It is for us — the people who build, maintain, and evolve the system. When a new developer joins the team, they learn the domain by reading the code. When the business changes a rule, we find the relevant code by following the vocabulary.

This idea is deeply connected to the notion of a bounded context. A bounded context is a central pattern in Domain-Driven Design. It defines the boundary within which a particular domain model and its vocabulary apply. Within a banking context, the word "balance" might mean the current amount of money in an account. But within an accounting context, "balance" might refer to the equality of debits and credits. The same word has different meanings in different contexts.

Bounded contexts help us manage complexity by ensuring that each part of the system has a clear, consistent vocabulary. They prevent the confusion that arises when we try to create a single, unified vocabulary for the entire domain.

Programming Languages as Thinking Tools

Different programming languages encourage different ways of thinking. Object-oriented languages like Java encourage thinking in terms of objects and their interactions. Functional languages like Haskell encourage thinking in terms of functions and compositions. Logic languages like Prolog encourage thinking in terms of relations and constraints.

This is not accidental. Programming languages are designed with particular paradigms in mind, and these paradigms shape the way we approach problems. When we choose a language, we are not just choosing a syntax. We are choosing a way of thinking.

This is why polyglot programming is valuable. By learning multiple languages, we expose ourselves to multiple ways of thinking. We become better problem solvers because we have a richer vocabulary of conceptual models to draw from.

Code as an Executable Specification

The other side of code is what it does. Code is not just a description of a domain — it is a description that a machine can execute. This is what makes code fundamentally different from other forms of documentation. A document can describe a business process, but only code can actually carry it out.

This dual nature of code — as both vocabulary and executable specification — is what gives it its power. When we write code, we are simultaneously documenting our understanding of a domain and creating a machine that enforces that understanding.

Working with LLMs

Large Language Models have introduced a new dimension to the relationship between code and thought. When we write code with LLMs, we are engaging in a dialogue where the LLM generates code based on our prompts, and we review, refine, and integrate that code into our systems.

This raises an interesting question: if the LLM is writing the code, do we still need to understand the vocabulary? The answer is a resounding yes. The LLM can generate code, but it cannot understand the bounded context of your specific domain. It does not know what "balance" means in your banking system versus your accounting system. It does not know the difference between a customer and a user in your e-commerce platform.

The vocabulary of code — the conceptual model it encodes — is still our responsibility. The LLM can help us express that vocabulary more efficiently, but it cannot replace the thinking that goes into defining it.

Cognitive Debt

This leads to the concept of cognitive debt. Cognitive debt is the cost of not understanding the code we work with. When we use LLMs to generate code without understanding the vocabulary, we accumulate cognitive debt. We have code that works, but we do not understand why it works or what it says about the domain.

This is different from technical debt, which is the cost of taking shortcuts in implementation. Cognitive debt is about the cost of not investing in understanding. It is the difference between having a working system and having a system that we can confidently evolve and maintain.

Cognitive debt is particularly insidious because it is invisible. Technical debt often manifests as bugs, performance issues, or difficulty adding features. Cognitive debt manifests as a growing inability to make informed decisions about the system. It is the slow erosion of understanding that makes the system increasingly opaque to its maintainers.

Code as a Shared Conceptual Model

When a team works on a codebase, the code becomes a shared conceptual model. It is the common language that the team uses to communicate about the domain. When a developer writes a class called "Account," they are not just defining a data structure. They are contributing to a shared understanding of what an account is in the context of this system.

This shared model evolves over time. As the team learns more about the domain, they refactor the code to reflect their improved understanding. This is why refactoring is not just about cleaning up code — it is about refining the vocabulary, improving the conceptual model, and strengthening the shared understanding of the team.

When a new developer joins the team, they learn the domain by reading the code. The code is their textbook. If the code does not accurately reflect the domain, the new developer will build an incorrect mental model, leading to mistakes and confusion.

Code Itself as Harness and Context

In the age of AI-assisted development, code takes on an additional role: it serves as harness and context for AI tools. The code we write — the vocabulary we define, the patterns we establish, the architecture we choose — all of this becomes the context within which AI operates.

When an LLM generates code, it does so based on the context we provide. If our code has a clear, consistent vocabulary, the LLM is more likely to generate code that fits within that vocabulary. If our code is a tangled mess of inconsistent concepts, the LLM will generate more of the same.

This is why the quality of our code matters more than ever. It is not just about maintainability or performance. It is about the quality of the context we provide to AI tools. Good code produces good AI suggestions. Bad code produces bad AI suggestions.

Conclusion

Code is more than instructions for a machine. It is a vocabulary for thought, a shared conceptual model, and the foundation upon which we build our understanding of complex domains. Whether we are writing it ourselves or collaborating with AI, the quality of our code reflects the quality of our thinking.

As we navigate the evolving landscape of software development, let us not lose sight of this fundamental truth: code is not just what we write. It is how we think. And how we think determines what we build.
